content.text: Distributed tracing for multi-agent runs with OpenTelemetry

is the practice of attaching a single trace ID to an entire agent workflow and propagating that ID across every service, worker, tool call, and LLM request so you can see the full request path from user input to final output. In a distributed multi-agent swarm, a single user task often touches many components (orchestrator, specialist agents, task queue, Redis, database, LLM API), and OpenTelemetry lets you stitch those spans together into one coherent timeline instead of chasing logs across five different systems.

When the orchestrator receives a request, it creates a trace:

# orchestrator.py
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.trace import set_span_in_context

tracer = trace.get_tracer("orchestrator")

def handle_request(user_id: str, goal: str):
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("orchestrator_root") as root_span:
        root_span.set_attribute("user_id", user_id)
        root_span.set_attribute("goal", goal)
        
        # Propagate trace to child spans
        context = set_span_in_context(root_span)
        
        # Spawn specialist agents
        for subtask in subtasks:
            spawn_agent(subtask, context)  # passes trace context
Each specialist agent receives the trace context and creates child spans:

# agent_worker.py
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.trace import get_current_span

tracer = trace.get_tracer("agent_worker")

def spawn_agent(subtask: dict, context):
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("scraper_agent", context=context) as span:
        span.set_attribute("domain", subtask["domain"])
        
        # Call LLM
        call_llm(context)  # passes trace context
The LLM call creates a leaf span:

# llm_client.py
from opentelemetry import trace

tracer = trace.get_tracer("llm_client")

def call_llm(context):
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("claude_llm", context=context) as span:
        span.set_attribute("model", "claude-3.5")
        span.set_attribute("tokens", 1000)
        
        response = llm_api.call(...)
        span.set_attribute("status", "success")
All spans share the same trace ID (
OpenTelemetry exports traces to a backend (e.g., Jaeger, Tempo, Datadog, Honeycomb):

# otel_config.py
from opentelemetry.exporter.jaeger.thrift import JaegerExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor

provider = TracerProvider()
processor = BatchSpanProcessor(JaegerExporter(endpoint="http://jaeger:14268/api/traces"))
provider.add_span_processor(processor)
trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)
This sends all spans to Jaeger, where you can query by trace ID.
In Jaeger/Datadog, query by trace ID:

: Root span → child spans → leaf spans.
-
: Which span failed and the error message.

┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  User Request                                             │
│  ↓                                                        │
│  Orchestrator (creates trace, propagates context)        │
│  ↓                                                        │
│  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐   │
│  │ Scraper Agent│  │ Analyst Agent│  │ Writer Agent │   │
│  │ (child span) │  │ (child span) │  │ (child span) │   │
│  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────┘   │
│       ↓                 ↓                    ↓           │
│  LLM API (leaf span: claude-3.5, tokens=1000)          │
│       ↓                 ↓                    ↓           │
│  OpenTelemetry Collector                                │
│  ↓                                                        │
│  Tracing Backend (Jaeger, Tempo, Datadog, Honeycomb)   │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
All components export spans to the same backend, where you can query by trace ID.
: One trace shows the entire workflow.
: See how long each step took.
: See which span failed and why.
: See tokens spent per step, per agent, per user.
: OpenTelemetry works across any language or framework.

one trace ID across all services
for creating, propagating, and exporting traces.
Understanding the internals of distributed tracing for multi-agent runs (opentelemetry) helps you predict its behavior and debug issues effectively.

When a request is processed, several layers of the system interact: the client library validates inputs and manages retries, the transport layer handles HTTP/2 connection multiplexing, and the API server routes to the appropriate model instance. Each layer introduces latency that you can measure and optimize.

For production deployments, understanding this pipeline helps you allocate timeout budgets appropriately. The network round-trip typically adds 50-100ms, while model processing time scales with output length. A response generating 1,000 tokens takes 2-4x longer than one generating 250 tokens for the same model.
Production experience with distributed tracing for multi-agent runs (opentelemetry) has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying distributed tracing for multi-agent runs (opentelemetry) in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Distributed tracing for multi-agent runs (OpenTelemetry) is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.

Distributed tracing for multi-agent runs (OpenTelemetry) is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
OpenTelemetry Instrumentation for Claude API Calls

Instrumenting the Anthropic client with OpenTelemetry requires wrapping each API call in a span that captures the full lifecycle: request construction, network round-trip, and response parsing. The span should carry semantic attributes that make traces useful across agents: the model name, the number of input and output tokens, the stop reason, which tools were invoked, and whether tool_use blocks appear in the response. These attributes let you write Jaeger queries like 'show all traces where claude.output_tokens > 3000' to find expensive calls.

OTel span status maps naturally to Claude API outcomes. A successful response with stop_reason='end_turn' or stop_reason='tool_use' should set SpanStatusCode.OK. An API error (network failure, rate limit, overload) should set SpanStatusCode.ERROR and record the exception on the span using span.record_exception. Content policy refusals are trickier — they return HTTP 200 but contain a stop_reason of 'stop' with no assistant content; model these as OK spans with an attribute claude.refusal=True so they are filterable without appearing as errors in your SLO dashboards.

Install the OTEL SDK with: pip install opentelemetry-sdk opentelemetry-api opentelemetry-exporter-otlp-proto-grpc. For local development, run Jaeger all-in-one via Docker and point the OTLP exporter at localhost:4317. The tracer provider should be initialised once at application startup, before any agent code runs. Using the global tracer provider (trace.set_tracer_provider) means individual agent modules can call trace.get_tracer(__name__) without depending on a shared singleton.

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.trace import SpanStatusCode
import anthropic
import functools


def configure_otel(service_name: str, otlp_endpoint: str = "http://localhost:4317"):
    provider = TracerProvider(
        resource=Resource({"service.name": service_name})
    )
    provider.add_span_processor(
        BatchSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint=otlp_endpoint))
    )
    trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)


tracer = trace.get_tracer("claude_agent")


def instrument_claude_call(func):
    """Decorator that wraps any async function calling the Anthropic API in a span."""
    @functools.wraps(func)
    async def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
        model = kwargs.get("model", "claude-opus-4-5")
        with tracer.start_as_current_span(
            "claude.messages.create",
            kind=trace.SpanKind.CLIENT,
        ) as span:
            span.set_attribute("claude.model", model)
            span.set_attribute("claude.max_tokens", kwargs.get("max_tokens", 0))
            tool_names = [t.get("name", "") for t in kwargs.get("tools", [])]
            span.set_attribute("claude.tools", ",".join(tool_names))
            try:
                response = await func(*args, **kwargs)
                span.set_attribute("claude.input_tokens", response.usage.input_tokens)
                span.set_attribute("claude.output_tokens", response.usage.output_tokens)
                span.set_attribute("claude.stop_reason", response.stop_reason)
                has_tool_use = any(
                    b.type == "tool_use" for b in response.content
                )
                span.set_attribute("claude.has_tool_use", has_tool_use)
                span.set_status(SpanStatusCode.OK)
                return response
            except anthropic.RateLimitError as exc:
                span.record_exception(exc)
                span.set_status(SpanStatusCode.ERROR, "rate_limit")
                raise
            except Exception as exc:
                span.record_exception(exc)
                span.set_status(SpanStatusCode.ERROR, str(exc))
                raise
    return wrapper

Context propagation is what turns a collection of individual spans into a connected trace. When an orchestrator agent spawns a specialist agent, the trace context (trace_id + parent span_id) must travel with the work unit so the specialist's spans appear as children of the orchestrator's span in Jaeger. OpenTelemetry's W3C TraceContext format encodes this context as two HTTP headers: traceparent (version-trace_id-parent_id-flags) and tracestate (vendor-specific key-value pairs). Inject these headers into every outbound call, and extract them on the receiving side before starting any spans.

The pattern is different for each transport layer. For synchronous HTTP calls between agent services, use OTel's inject/extract API with the HTTP header propagator. For Celery task queues, inject context into the task's kwargs dict before submission and extract it inside the task worker. For asyncio queues (asyncio.Queue), serialize the context as a dict and pass it alongside the work payload. For Redis-based queues, store the context as additional metadata fields in the message. The key mistake to avoid is assuming context propagates automatically — it does not cross process or thread boundaries unless you explicitly inject and extract it.

Baggage propagation is complementary to trace context. Where TraceContext carries only IDs, W3C Baggage carries named key-value pairs that propagate along with the trace. Use baggage for cross-cutting concerns like user_id, tenant_id, or deployment_version that you want available in every span without having to re-set them at each instrumentation point. Keep baggage small (fewer than 10 keys, values under 256 bytes) because it is transmitted in every request header.

import httpx
from opentelemetry import trace, propagate, baggage
from opentelemetry.propagators.b3 import B3MultiFormat  # alternative to W3C
from opentelemetry.trace.propagation.tracecontext import TraceContextTextMapPropagator

propagate.set_global_textmap(TraceContextTextMapPropagator())
tracer = trace.get_tracer("orchestrator")


async def call_specialist_agent_http(
    url: str, payload: dict, user_id: str, tenant_id: str
) -> dict:
    """HTTP call to a specialist agent with full trace + baggage propagation."""
    headers = {}
    # Inject W3C traceparent + tracestate headers
    propagate.inject(headers)
    # Inject baggage for cross-cutting metadata
    ctx = baggage.set_baggage("user_id", user_id)
    ctx = baggage.set_baggage("tenant_id", tenant_id, context=ctx)
    propagate.inject(headers, context=ctx)

    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        resp = await client.post(url, json=payload, headers=headers, timeout=30)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        return resp.json()


# In the specialist agent service (FastAPI example):
from fastapi import Request
from opentelemetry.instrumentation.fastapi import FastAPIInstrumentor

def extract_context_middleware(request: Request):
    """Extract trace context from inbound request headers."""
    ctx = propagate.extract(dict(request.headers))
    user_id = baggage.get_baggage("user_id", context=ctx)
    tenant_id = baggage.get_baggage("tenant_id", context=ctx)
    return ctx, user_id, tenant_id
Sampling Strategies for High-Volume Agent Systems

Collecting 100% of traces is the right starting point for new agent deployments, but it becomes cost-prohibitive at scale. A single multi-agent run touching 10 agents with 5 LLM calls each generates ~60 spans. At 1,000 concurrent runs, that is 60,000 spans per minute. Most tracing backends charge by ingested span volume; at Datadog or Honeycomb pricing, this easily reaches thousands of dollars per month. Sampling reduces volume while preserving the ability to investigate problems.

Head-based sampling makes the keep/drop decision at the root span before any child spans exist. It is low-overhead but misses the ability to keep traces that turn out to be interesting (for example, an error that occurs deep in the trace). Tail-based sampling buffers all spans for a trace and decides whether to keep it after the trace is complete, allowing error-rate-based sampling (always keep error traces, sample successful ones at 10%). The OTEL Collector's tail_sampling processor implements this. For most agent systems, a hybrid approach works well: 100% for error traces, 100% for traces involving specific high-value actions (policy_check, security-sensitive tools), and 10% for routine LLM invocations.

The ParentBased sampler is the correct default for distributed systems: it respects the sampling decision made by the root span so all spans in a trace are either kept or dropped together. Without ParentBased, a downstream agent might drop spans even though the orchestrator decided to sample the trace, producing broken traces with missing spans.
Head-based sampling: low overhead, decided at trace start; misses late-manifesting errors
Tail-based sampling: buffer full trace then decide; catches errors but requires OTEL Collector
Always sample: error traces, security events, policy checks, user-reported incidents
Sample at 1-10%: routine successful LLM invocations in high-throughput pipelines
Never sample: drop health check spans and infra noise with a FilterSampler before ratio sampling
